title: Paper 2 — reading comprehension strategies
summary: This lesson provides an in-depth guide to mastering the IB Spanish B SL Paper 2 reading comprehension. It details a systematic, three-phase strategy for tackling texts, breaks down different question types with worked examples, and highlights common pitfalls to avoid, all aligned with official IB assessment criteria.
learningObjectives: Identify and deconstruct various question types found in Paper 2, such as multiple-choice, true/false with justification, and open-ended questions.
Apply strategic reading techniques, including skimming for gist, scanning for specific information, and detailed reading for nuance.
Analyse textual features to infer meaning, purpose, and tone, moving beyond literal comprehension.
Formulate precise and concise answers that directly address the question and are fully supported by textual evidence, as required by the markscheme.
Recognise and avoid common errors, such as relying on outside knowledge or providing inaccurate justifications, to maximise marks under Criterion A.

simpleExplanation.title: Decoding Paper 2: From Text to Top Marks
simpleExplanation.summary: Paper 2 tests your ability to strategically extract specific information from unseen Spanish texts under time pressure. Success isn't just about understanding the language; it's about applying a methodical approach to find and present the exact answers the examiner is looking for.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of Paper 2 as being a detective at a crime scene. The text is the scene, and the questions are the specific clues you need to find. You wouldn't read every book in the house to find a footprint. Instead, you'd first survey the scene (skim the text), then look for specific evidence like footprints or fingerprints (scan for keywords), and finally, analyse the evidence closely (detailed reading) to solve the case (answer the question).
steps: Pre-read (1-2 mins): Before reading the text, read the title, source, and all the questions. This primes your brain and tells you what specific information you need to find.
First Read (Skimming): Read the text quickly (ojear) to understand its general theme and structure (la idea principal). Don't get stuck on unfamiliar words.

Targeted Reading (Scanning & Detailed): Go back to each question. Scan (escanear) the text for keywords related to the question to locate the relevant section. Then, perform a detailed read of that specific part to formulate your answer.

Answer & Justify: Write your answer using information ONLY from the text. For 'justifica tu respuesta' questions, copy the exact phrase or sentence from the text that proves your point. Do not add your own opinions.

flashcards.back: Reading comprehension. The sole focus of Paper 2, assessed under Criterion A.
To justify. In Paper 2, this means providing a direct quote or precise reference from the text to support your answer.
To infer. To deduce or conclude information that is not explicitly stated in the text, based on the evidence provided.
The main idea or gist of a text or paragraph. Identified by skimming.
The author's purpose (e.g., to inform, persuade, entertain). You may be asked to infer this from the tone and content.
Keywords. Important words in a question that you should scan for in the text to locate the relevant information.
To skim. The strategy of reading quickly to get a general overview of a text.
To scan. The strategy of quickly searching a text for a specific piece of information (like a name, date, or keyword).
An incorrect option in a multiple-choice question that is designed to seem plausible. It often uses words from the text but changes the meaning.
True / False. A common question type, often paired with a justification. You must get the V/F part correct to be awarded marks for the justification.
A direct quote. The most reliable way to provide a justification in Paper 2.
According to the text... This phrase is a reminder that all answers must be based exclusively on the information provided in the passage, not your own knowledge.

faq.q: Do I lose marks for spelling or grammar mistakes in my answers on Paper 2?
faq.a: No, Criterion A (Comprensión) does not directly penalise you for language errors in your answers, as long as your meaning is clear and the examiner can understand what you are trying to say. The focus is purely on your understanding of the text. However, if your errors are so significant that they obscure the meaning of your answer, you may not be awarded the mark.

faq.q: How much should I write for open-ended questions?
faq.a: Be as concise and direct as possible. The space provided on the exam paper is a reliable guide for the expected length of the answer. There are no extra marks for writing more; marks are awarded for accuracy and relevance. Often, a single, well-constructed sentence is sufficient to secure the marks.

faq.q: What should I do if I absolutely cannot find the answer to a question?
faq.a: Do not waste too much time on a single question, as this can compromise your performance on the rest of the paper. Make an educated guess if you can, but otherwise, mark the question, move on to the next ones, and come back to it at the very end if you have time. Answering other questions might give you a better overall understanding of the text, which could help you find the answer you were initially looking for.

sections.content: Welcome to your guide on mastering Paper 2 for Spanish B SL. This paper assesses your reading comprehension skills (Criterion A: Comprensión) and accounts for a significant portion of your final grade. This lesson will equip you with a robust, strategic framework to confidently navigate the texts, understand the questions, and secure top marks by thinking like an examiner.
Understanding the Assessment: Paper 2 Structure and Criteria

Paper 2 is a 1-hour 45-minute examination consisting of three reading comprehension texts, each related to one of the five prescribed themes: Identidades, Experiencias, Ingenio humano, Organización social, or Cómo compartimos el planeta. The entire paper is marked out of 45, all under Criterion A: Comprensión. This means every single mark is awarded for demonstrating your understanding of the texts. There are no marks for language production, but your answers must be clear enough for the examiner to understand. The texts will vary in style and may include articles, interviews, blogs, or reports.

items: Structure: 3 texts with a series of comprehension questions.
Themes: Texts are drawn from the 5 prescribed IB themes.
Key Skill: Demonstrating understanding through accurate responses based solely on the text.

sections.content: The Three-Phase Reading Strategy: Before, During, and After

A systematic approach is more effective than simply reading the text from start to finish and then attempting the questions. By breaking the process into three phases, you can read with purpose and efficiency.

items: Phase 1 (Pre-lectura): Before reading the main body of the text, spend 1-2 minutes reading the title, the source information, and, most importantly, all the questions. This activates your knowledge of the topic and transforms your reading from a passive act to an active search for specific information.

Phase 2 (Lectura Activa): First, skim the text to grasp the main idea. Then, using keywords from the questions, scan the text to locate the relevant paragraphs or sentences. Once located, switch to a detailed, careful reading of that specific section to ensure you understand the nuances before formulating your answer. Underline key phrases as you go.

Phase 3 (Post-lectura): This is the answering phase. Formulate your answers concisely, using the text as your only source of information. For justification questions, a direct quote is often the safest and most effective method. After answering all questions for a text, do a quick check to ensure you haven't misinterpreted anything.

sections.question:
Texto de ejemplo (fragmento): 'El teletrabajo ofrece una flexibilidad innegable, permitiendo a los empleados organizar sus horarios. Sin embargo, estudios recientes indican que la falta de separación física entre el hogar y la oficina puede conducir al agotamiento digital, ya que los empleados sienten la presión de estar conectados constantemente.'

Pregunta: El teletrabajo siempre tiene un impacto positivo en el bienestar del empleado. (Verdadero/Falso). Justifica tu respuesta con una frase del texto.
sections.solution:
Respuesta: Falso.

Justificación: "...la falta de separación física entre el hogar y la oficina puede conducir al agotamiento digital..."

Análisis del examinador: The candidate correctly identifies the statement as 'Falso' (1 mark). The justification provided is a direct and accurate quote from the text that explicitly contradicts the statement, proving that the impact is not 'siempre positivo'. This demonstrates excellent comprehension and adherence to the task requirements (1 mark). The candidate has successfully isolated the contrasting idea introduced by 'Sin embargo'.

sections.content: For 'justificación' questions, you are awarded two separate marks: one for the correct initial choice (e.g., Verdadero/Falso, or selecting the right option from a list) and one for the supporting evidence. You cannot get the mark for the justification if the first part is wrong. To be safe, lift the justification directly from the text as a quote unless the question specifies 'en tus propias palabras'.
The exam uses a variety of question formats to test your comprehension in different ways. Understanding how to approach each type is crucial.

items: Opción Múltiple: Be wary of distractors. These are options that use words from the text but are factually incorrect or misleading. The correct answer is often a paraphrase of the information in the text, not a word-for-word match.
Completar la frase: The word or phrase you choose must fit both grammatically and contextually. After filling the blank, read the complete sentence to ensure it flows logically and makes sense.

Encuentra la palabra/expresión: When asked to find a word or synonym, ensure you take it from the specific line or paragraph indicated in the question. The word must match the meaning given in the context of the passage.

Preguntas abiertas (e.g., ¿Por qué...? ¿Qué efecto...?): Answer concisely and directly. Use the wording of the question to begin your answer (e.g., Q: "¿Por qué se canceló el evento?" A: "El evento se canceló porque..."). The space provided on the exam paper is a good indicator of the expected length.

sections.question:
Texto de ejemplo (fragmento): 'El nuevo centro cívico, financiado por donaciones locales, abrió sus puertas el sábado. Durante la inauguración, los vecinos, muchos de los cuales no se conocían previamente, compartieron historias y planearon futuras actividades conjuntas. La atmósfera era de una alegría palpable.'

Pregunta: Según el texto, ¿qué se puede inferir sobre el impacto del centro cívico en la comunidad, más allá de ser un nuevo edificio?
sections.solution:
Respuesta modelo: Se puede inferir que el centro cívico está fomentando un sentido de comunidad y fortaleciendo las relaciones sociales entre los vecinos.

Análisis del examinador: This is a high-level response. The text does not explicitly state 'fomentando un sentido de comunidad'. Instead, the candidate has correctly inferred this abstract concept from the concrete evidence provided: 'vecinos... no se conocían previamente', 'compartieron historias' and 'planearon futuras actividades'. This demonstrates the ability to read between the lines, a key skill for achieving top marks in questions with command terms like 'inferir'.

sections.content: Common Pitfalls and How to Avoid Them
Many students lose marks not due to a lack of Spanish knowledge, but due to common, avoidable exam errors. Being aware of these traps is the first step to avoiding them.

items: Using Outside Knowledge: Your general knowledge is irrelevant. If the text states something that is factually incorrect in the real world, you must answer based on what the text says. Every answer must be traceable back to the passage.
Misreading the Question: Underline the command terms and key elements of the question. If it asks for 'dos razones', provide exactly two. If it asks 'en la primera parte del texto', only look there.
Vague Justifications: Simply quoting the entire paragraph is not a justification. You must pinpoint the specific phrase or sentence that directly supports your answer.

Poor Time Management: Allocate your time wisely. A rough guide is 35 minutes per text. If you get stuck on a question, mark it, move on, and return to it later if you have time. It's better to attempt all questions than to perfect one section and run out of time.

sections.content: Don't panic if you encounter an unfamiliar word. First, see if you can answer the question without knowing it. If not, try to deduce its meaning from the context. Look at the words around it, consider the overall topic of the paragraph, and see if the word looks similar to an English or French word you know. Often, a precise definition isn't necessary to understand the point.

sections.label: Test your comprehension skills with an exam-style text and questions
